coredata.dc:title: Electrocatalytic activity of manganese tetra 4-aminophenyl porphyrin in the presence of graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Jokazi, Mbulelo
Mpeta, Lekhetho S.
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description:
A manganese tetra 4-aminophenyl porphyrin (MnTAPP) was synthesized and attached to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via covalent and
π - π
interaction to give MnTAPP@GQDs and MnTAPP--GQDs, respectively. There was an increase (using dynamic light scattering and transmission electron microscopy) in size of the GQDs in the presence of the porphyrin due to aggregation. The porphyrins in the absence and presence of GQDs were then adsorbed onto a glassy carbon electrode using drop and dry method. The electrochemical behavior was tested by cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry towards hydrazine. Both methods of combining MnTAPP with GQDs improved the activity of the electrocatalysis compared to individual components. MnTAPP@GQDs showed better catalytic rate constant of 4.36 × 102 Ms−1 and lowest LoD of 0.0023 mM followed by MnTAPP--GQDs. Furthermore, the sensor showed good selectivity in the presence of interfering analytes. All probes showed good stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Manganese porphyrin
Graphene quantum dots
Hydrazine
Electrocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of porphyrin, GQDs and their combination
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and EIS in ferricyanide

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–Vis spectra of TAPP, MnTAPP, GQDs, MnTAPP@GQDs, and MnTAPP--GQDs. All in DMF except for GQDs alone which is in water.
DLS measurements for A) MnTAPP, B) GQDs, C) MnTAPP@GQDs, and D) MnTAPP--GQDs.
TEM images of a) GQDs, b) MnTAPP@GQDs, and c) MnTAPP--GQDs.
XPS high resolution N1s of MnTAPP and MnTAPP@GQDs.
FTIR spectra for GQDs, MnTAPP and MnTAPP@GQDs.
Cyclic voltammograms of bare electrode, TAPP, GQDs, MnTAPP, MnTAPP@GQDs, and MnTAPP--GQDs at 100 mV/s in 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 01 M KCl.
a) Nyquist plots of various electrodes: bare, TAPP, GQDs, MnTAPP, MnTAPP@GQDs, and MnTAPP--GQDs in 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 01 M KCl. b) Randles model used for fitting.
Cyclic Voltammograms of bare electrode, TAPP, MnTAPP, GQDs, MnTAPP@GQDs, and MnTAPP--GQDs at 100 mV/s in 0.1 M NaOH. Insert: expansion to show peaks near −0.5 better.
Cyclic voltammograms of bare electrode, TAPP, MnTAPP, GQDs, MnTAPP@GQDs, and MnTAPP--GQDs at 100 mV/s in 0.1 M NaOH containing 1 mM Hydrazine.
Representation of covalent linking (A) and π-π interaction (B) of MnTAPP and GQDs.
Electrochemical parameters of the modified electrodes.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Where a = tetra phenyl porphyrin/reduced graphene oxide, b = Ruthenium oxide nanocomposite and c = Cobalt Octaethylporphyrin

head.ce:title: Electrocatalytic activity of manganese tetra 4-aminophenyl porphyrin in the presence of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A manganese tetra 4-aminophenyl porphyrin (MnTAPP) was synthesized and attached to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via covalent and
π - π
interaction to give MnTAPP@GQDs and MnTAPP--GQDs, respectively. There was an increase (using dynamic light scattering and transmission electron microscopy) in size of the GQDs in the presence of the porphyrin due to aggregation. The porphyrins in the absence and presence of GQDs were then adsorbed onto a glassy carbon electrode using drop and dry method. The electrochemical behavior was tested by cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry towards hydrazine. Both methods of combining MnTAPP with GQDs improved the activity of the electrocatalysis compared to individual components. MnTAPP@GQDs showed better catalytic rate constant of 4.36 × 102 Ms−1 and lowest LoD of 0.0023 mM followed by MnTAPP--GQDs. Furthermore, the sensor showed good selectivity in the presence of interfering analytes. All probes showed good stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been widely studied due to their exceptional electrical, optical and optoelectrical properties [1–3]. Recent studies have showed that GQDs have properties that are useful in electrochemistry such as good electrocatalytic activity, high speed electron transport and have a controllable synthesis [2,4]. In electrochemistry, GQDs are mostly used to modify working electrodes to improve electrochemical sensing [5]. In this work, GQDs are employed together with porphyrins for electrochemical sensing.

Metalloporphyrins are known electrocatalysts for many analytes [6]. The catalytic activity of metal porphyrins is related to the nature of the central metal [7,8]. Metalloporphyrins containing electroactive central metal such Mn show good electrocatalytic activity. In addition, substituents and axial ligands also play a significant role in electrocatalysis [7–10]. Electron donating groups as substituents in porphyrins will result in easy oxidation and improve electrocatalytic activity towards oxidation, hence a Mn tetra 4-aminophenyl porphyrin (MnTAPP) is employed in this work. In literature metal porphyrins have been proven to be effective electrocatalysts for the oxidation of hydrazine [10,11]. Most reports are on the catalytic oxidation of hydrazine using polymerized porphyrins [6,11]. Under the same conditions, unmetalated porphyrin complexes gave lower oxidation potentials for hydrazine oxidation than Co or Mn porphyrin complexes [6], hence both free base and Mn porphyrins are employed in this work. We show in this work that MnTAPP performs better (in terms of limits of detection) than the free base TAPP, and thus we show the importance of the central metal. Mn was chosen as a central metal since it has several oxidation states which is expected to enhance electrocatalysis.

GQDs have been employed together with porphyrin-type complexes such as phthalocyanines for electrocatalysis [12,13]. However, this is the first study on the electrocatalytic activity of porphyrins in the presence of GQDs. MnTAPP has been used in the presence of graphene oxide for electrocatalysis [14]. MnTAPP is employed for the first time together with GQDs since the latter show high performance and stability which is attributed to large surface-to-volume ratio and excellent biocompatibility [15].

The main objective of this study is to report on the electrocatalytic behavior of MnTAPP in the presence of GQDs for the detection of hydrazine. The MnTAPP was attached to GQDs via
π - π
stacking, Scheme 1 , which is possible since both the porphyrins and the GQDs have π electrons. MnTAPP was also linked to GQDs by covalent linkage, Scheme 1. The probes are represented as MnTAPP@GQDs for covalently linked and MnTAPP--GQDs for
π - π
interaction.

Hydrazine is employed as a test analyte which is used in the pharmaceutical and chemical industry [16]. However, hydrazine and its derivatives are recognized as environmental pollutants [10], hence the study of sensitive methods for the detection of hydrazine is very important.

N, N dimethyl formamide (DMF) was purchased from Merck. N-hydroxysuccinamide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), potassium ferricyanide, potassium ferrocyanide, potassium chloride, hydrazine monohydrate, and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Type II water from ELGA Lab Water was used for the aqueous solutions. TAPP and MnTAPP were synthesized as reported in literature [17,18] and gave satisfactory characterization data. The synthesis of GQDs has been reported before [19].
UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained from a Shimadzu UV-2250 spectrophotometer. Infrared (IR) spectra were recorded with a Bruker Alpha IR (100 FT-IR) spectrophotometer.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis for the characterisation of covalently linked conjugates was done using an AXIS Ultra DLD (supplied by Kratos Analytical) using Al (monochromatic) anode equipped with a charge neutralizer, the following parameters were used: the emission was 10 mA, the anode (HT) was 15 kV and the operating pressure below 5 × 10−9 Torr. A hybrid lens was used and resolution to acquire scans was at 160 eV pass energy in slot mode. The center used for the scans was at 520 eV (width of 1205 eV) with steps at 1 eV and dwell time at 100 ms. The high-resolution scans were acquired using 80 eV pass energy in slot mode. The chemically distinct peaks were resolved using a non-linear least squares curve fitting procedure. The core level binding energies (BEs) were aligned with respect to the C 1 s binding energy (BE) of 284.5 eV.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were performed on a Bruker D8 Discover diffractometer, equipped with a LynxEye detector, under Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were done on a Malvern Zetasizer nanoseries, Nano-ZS90. Transmission electron spectroscopy (TEM) images were obtained from Zeiss Libra TEM 120 Model operated at 90 kV. Cyclic voltammetric, square wave voltammetry, chronoamperometry, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data were recorded using an Autolab Potentiostat PGSTAT30 equipped with Nova software version 2.1.

The interactions of the porphyrin with the GQDs were through
π - π
stacking and covalent linkage, following the procedure reported in literature [20].

For
π - π
interaction, MnTAPP (12 mg, 0.016 mmol) was dissolved in DMF (3 mL), followed by addition of GQDs solution (10 mg/3mL) in water. The mixture was sonicated for 4 h at room temperature and then stirred for 24 h. The product was precipitated with ethanol and washed several times with chloroform and water, and was dried to give MnTAPP--GQDs (Scheme 1). TAPP was not linked to GQDs since as we will show below, it shows poor performance.

Covalently linked conjugate was prepared as follows: GQDs (7 mg) were dissolved in 6 mL water, then NHS (1.5 mg, 0.013 mmol), and EDC (1.5 mg, 0.0097 mmol) were added to the solution to activate the carboxylic acid groups of the GQDs. The mixture was left to stir for 24 h. MnTAPP (7 mg, 0.0096 mmol) in DMF (3 mL) was added to the mixture which was left to stir for another 24 h. The product was precipitated with methanol. The precipitate was centrifuged and washed with water, methanol and chloroform. The conjugate is represented as MnTAPP@GQDs.

The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as a working electrode, platinum wire as a counter electrode and silver|silver chloride (3 M KCl) as the reference electrode. The GCE was polished to a silver mirror on a Buehler felt pad using alumina (<10 µm) and washed with Millipore water and then sonicated for 5 min to remove impurities. The electrode was modified using the drop-and-dry method. The modification of electrodes was as follows: 5 µL (1 mg/mL) each of TAPP, MnTAPP, GQDs, MnTAPP--GQDs, and MnTAPP@GQDs, were dropped on the GCE and dried at 70 °C. The modified electrodes were then used to perform cyclic voltammetry, EIS, and chronoamperometry. Solutions for electrochemical experiments were deaerated by bubbling argon gas prior to the experiments and the electrochemical cell was kept under argon atmosphere throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of porphyrin, GQDs and their combination
ce:section.ce:para: The attachment of MnTAPP to GQDs via covalent and non-covalent attachment is represented in Scheme 1

All free base porphyrins have a similar visible spectrum with four absorption bands (Q bands) between 500 and 700 nm and the Soret band between 400 and 500 nm [21]. The metalation results in the four Q bands collapsing into two and the Soret band shifting to the red [22]. In Fig. 1 (Table S1), there is a significant red shift of the Soret band following metalation. The presence of manganese metal was also easily detected by the presence of the bands at 355 nm, 395 nm, and 433 nm which are typical of Mn porphyrins and are assigned to charge transfer transitions [23]. In the presence of GQDs, a blue shift of the Soret band was observed in both
π - π
stacking and covalently linkage from 483 nm to 480 nm and 477 nm, respectively (Table S1). The blue shift is evidence of strong interaction between MnTAPP and GQDs and hence suggest that the linkage was successful [24]. The GQDs showed a sharp band at 334 nm.

DLS analysis Fig. 2 was used to determine the particle size of MnTAPP, GQDs and their combination. The hydrodynamic size of MnTAPP@GQDs and MnTAPP--GQDs was 31.69 nm and 32.68 nm. This was an increase in size compared to GQDs which had a size of 6.52 nm. TEM was also used to determine the size of GQDs when alone or in the presence of MnTAPP. The images are shown in Fig. 3 and the results correspond to the results found in DLS. The increase in the size of the GQDs after the attachment of MnTAPP could be due to aggregation by interaction of MnTAPP on adjacent GQDs. The TEM sizes for GQDs, MnTAPP--GQDs and MnTAPP
@
GQDs are 4.28 nm, 30.33 nm and 30.01 nm, respectively. Values are lower for TEM compared to DLS due to the interference of the dispersant into the hydrodynamic diameter for the latter [25].

A broad peak at 2Θ = 20° was obtained from the XRD analysis of GQDs (Fig. S1, Supporting information) due to (002) Bragg’s reflection of the carbon in the graphene layers [26]. MnTAPP displayed broad peaks that are typical of porphyrins [27]. Upon linking, the broad peaks of GQDs and MnTAPP are observed with a slight shift.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to confirm the amide linkage between MnTAPP and GQDs to give MnTAPP@GQDs (Fig. 4 ). The high-resolution spectrum N1s was deconvoluted into two components at 397.4 eV and 399.3 eV corresponding to NC and NH2 for MnTAPP (Fig. 4). N1s deconvolution for MnTAPP@GQDs also yielded two peaks at 398.2 eV and 399.1 eV corresponding to NC and NH-C(O) which confirmed the binding of carbonyl and the nitrogen forming amide bond. There are no differences in high resolution N1s XPS spectra for the non-covalently attached MnTAPP--GQDs compared to MnTAPP since there are no new bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and EIS in ferricyanide
ce:section.ce:para:
The CV scans of bare electrode of all probes in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− are shown in Fig. 6 . A peak separation
Δ
Ep closer to the Nernstian value 58 mV indicates a good electron transfer kinetics for the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− [31]. The obtained peak separations are in the order 69 mV < 291 mV < 324 mV < 371 mV < 414 mV < 517 mV for bare, GQDs, MnTAPP--GQDs, MnTAPP@GQDs, MnTAPP and TAPP respectively, Table S2. The smaller the
Δ
Ep, the better the electron transfer. Thus, the bare electrode shows good electron transfer behavior. Of the modified electrodes, GQDs has a better electron transfer ability with the lowest
Δ
Ep value of 291 mV as shown in Table S2. GQDs lowers the
Δ
Ep of the MnTAPP when the two are combined. MnTAPP--GQDs showed better electron transfer than MnTAPP@GQDs. As no bond formation takes place, non-covalent interaction preserves GQDs electronic properties, hence better electron transfer.

The active area of the electrodes was determined using the Randles-Sevcik Eqn.1 and using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl.
(1)
I p
= 2.69 ×
10
5
n
3 / 2
A C
D
1 2
v
1 / 2
where Ip = is the peak current, A = the effective surface area, C = concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4, D = diffusion coefficient 7.6 × 10−6 cm2/s of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 [32], n = the number of electrons involved, and v = scan rate. The calculated areas from Eqn.1 were found to be 0.078 cm2, 0.058 cm2, 0.044 cm2, 0.042 cm2, 0.040 cm2 for MnTAPP, MnTAPP@GQDs, MnTAPP--GQDs, TAPP, and GQDs respectively.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used study the redox structural features of the modified surface of electrode, Fig. 7 a. The electrochemical response of each electrode is recorded in terms of Rct. The Rct value is a measure of electron transfer resistance on an electrode. The Rct values obtained via Randles model (Fig. 7b) are provided in Table S2. The lower the Rct value the better the charge transfer kinetics. MnTAPP gave lower Rct value compared to TAPP due to the presence of manganese, moreover both MnTAPP--GQDs and MnTAPP@GQDs gave lower Rct values compared to MnTAPP due to the incorporation of GQDs. Furthermore MnTAPP--GQDs gave a slightly lower Rct value compared to MnTAP@GQDs. The EIS results correspond to peak separation
Δ
Ep which also indicates electron transfer kinetics.

Fig. 8 shows the CV of bare and modified electrodes in 0.1 M NaOH, to be used for the detection of hydrazine. Peaks observed from 0.3 to 0.7 V on MnTAPP or TAPP modified electrodes are due to the porphyrin ring since Mn(III) porphyrins do not show metal oxidation [33]. These peaks are not clear in the presence of GQDs. This could be due to the shielding of the MnTAPP by the GQDs. Peaks near −0.5 V for Mn(III)TAPP containing electrode may due to Mn(III)/Mn(II) processes [34] and are involved in the electrocatalytic oxidation using Mn porphyrins. Peaks in this region are typical of GQDs alone and hence are observed for GQDs alone [35]. These peaks are present but reduced in intensity when MnTAPP and GQDs are combined. Fig. S2 shows GQDs, MnTAPP, and MnTAPP@GQDs responses at a smaller potential window (-0.8 to + 0.8 V) to avoid the signals from oxygen evolution reaction as it is normally detected around + 0.8 V and above. The peak near −0.5 V was observed for all modifiers (Fig. 8, insert) and was employed for the calculation of surface coverages Eqn.2.
(2)
Γ =
Q
nFA
where Q is the integrated effective area of the electrode, n the number of electrons transferred, F the Faraday constant (96,485C mol−1), A is the effective surface area obtained from Eq. (1) and Г is the surface coverage. The surface coverage values on the electrode was found to be 1.01 × 10−7 mol.cm−2, 1.51 × 10−7 mol.cm−2, 1.09 × 10−7 mol.cm−2, 2.65 × 10−9 mol.cm−2, 2.79 × 10−10 mol.cm−2 for MnTAPP, MnTAPP--GQDs, MnTAPP@GQDs, GQDs, and TAPP respectively (Table S2). The MnTAPP--GQDs showed higher surface coverage compared to MnTAPP@GQDs and TAPP showed the lowest surface coverage compared to all probes. An increase in surface coverage increases electron transfer rate [36]. In the presence of GQDs there was an increase in surface coverage in both MnTAPP@GQDs and MnTAPP--GQDs compared to MnTAPP.

The cyclic voltammetric responses of modified glassy carbon electrodes in NaOH buffer with hydrazine are shown in Fig. 9 . The voltammograms are separated for clarity in Fig. S3. The anodic peak potential for the oxidation of hydrazine is observed but no cathodic peak is found, and this indicates irreversible behavior [37]. The peak potential observed ranged from 0.65 to 0.80 V for the detection of hydrazine, Table 1 . Hydrazine oxidation is 0.75 V in the presence of porphyrin alone (TAPP). A decrease of 0.10 V in peak potential with an increase in background current is observed in the presence of manganese as a central metal (MnTAPP) and this therefore shows an improvement in electrocatalytic activity for the metalated complex [38]. The catalytic activity of porphyrin on electrooxidation of hydrazine is determined by the central metal ion. The bare GCE showed no rsponse and GQDs showed the anodic peak potential of 0.80 V while MnTAPP@GQDs and MnTAPP--GQDs showed 0.68 V and 0.70 V under same conditions. Although the both MnTAPP@GQDs and MnTAPP--GQDs showed higher anodic peak potential compared to that of MnTAPP, MnTAPP@GQDs showed lower anodic peak potential (0.68 V) compared to MnTAPP--GQDs (0.70 V) and this could be due to the effect of the direct linkage of GQDs and the porphyrin. According to the previous studies, GQDs result in shifting the potential to the negative direction and this is because GQDs have high electron transfer rate [39]. Furthermore, no hydrogen formation was observed as there was no bubble formation during the experiment. This is an indication that there is no hydrolysis of hydrazine and therefore no loss in power. MnTAPP--GQDs and MnTAPP@GQDs showed the largest background corrected current in Table 1, corresponding to the large surface area for the two. MnTAPP--GQDs showed slightly lower catalytic current compared to MnTAPP@GQDs. High currents are more important as they play part in giving better limit of detection (LoD).

The anodic oxidation of hydrazine in aqueous solution has been widely studied for several years. The proposed mechanism (Eqs. 3–5) for hydrazine oxidation is similar to that reported for Mn phthalocyanines [36] as follows, equations 3–5
Mn(lll)TAPP + N2H4 → [Mn(ll)TAPP]−…[N2H4]+(3)
[Mn(ll)TAPP]−…[N2H4]+ + OH– ↔ Mn(lll)TAPP…N2H3 + H2O + e−(4)
Mn(lll)TAPP…N2H3 + OH– → H2O + Mn(lll)TAPP + N2H2 + e−(5)
Eq. (5) is rate determining step.

It has been reported that N2H4 further decomposes rapidly via two one-electron steps to give N2 which is the final product and also that Mn(lll)TAPP/Mn(ll)TAPP mediate the electrocatalysis of hydrazine [36]. These peaks were observed in the supporting electrolyte NaOH, Fig. 8.

The stability of all the complexes was tested by running 26 consecutive scans in 1 mM hydrazine (Fig. S4). There was only a small shift in potential but no decrease in current. The potential stabilized after 26 consecutive scans. The metal porphyrin (MnTAPP) showed better stability than (TAPP) whereas both MnTAPP@GQDs and MnTAPP--GQDs showed great results in stability. The electrodes were stored for 2 weeks and still showed stability.

Various scan rates were performed for the detection of hydrazine to determine the reversibility of the catalytic reaction (Fig. S5a). The effect of scan rates on peak current of hydrazine oxidation at different platforms was monitored by plotting peak current as a function of square root of scan rates and is shown in (Fig. S5c). The linear relationship between the current and the square root of scan rates indicates diffusion control [40–43]. Linear plots of Ep vs. Log v (Fig. S5b) were obtained and used to calculate Tafel slope with the help of Eqn.6.
(6)
E p
=
b 2
log v + K
where Ep is the potential, b is the Tafel slope, v is scan rate, and K is a constant. The Tafel slopes obtained for all the complexes are shown in Table 1. The Tafel slopes were all beyond the normal range (60–120 mV/decade), MnTAPP--GQDs showing the highest 740 mV/decade followed by MnTAPP which has 657 mV/decade. MnTAPP@GQDs showed a tafel slope of 344 mV/decade which is lower than MnTAPP--GQDs. Tafel slopes that are higher than the 120 mV/decade have no kinetic meaning and could be due to chemical complications in the reaction [43]. The Tafel slopes found for this study are all higher than 120 mV/decade. This indicates that the overall reaction mechanism is not simple most likely due to the presence various reactive species on electrode surface.

Chronoamperometry was employed to determine the catalytic rate constant and the limit of detection (LoD) of the complexes. Fig. S6a shows the chronoamperogram of MnTAPP@GQDs as an example. Plots of Icat/Ibuf vs. square root of time within rapid decay region (<2 s) were linear with varying slopes (Fig. S6b). The rate constant was determined using the Eqn. (7).
(7)
I
cat
I
buf
=
γ
1 2
π
1 2
=
π
1 2
( k C t )
1 2
where I cat and I buf currents on the modified electrodes in the presence and absence of hydrazine, k is the catalytic rate constant, C is the bulk concentration of hydrazine, and t the time elapsed in seconds. The slopes for the plots of I cat /I buf vs. square root of time were plotted against concentration of hydrazine Fig. S6c. The slope is equal to
π k
where k is rate constant. The linear relationships of the plots in Fig. S6c are represented by equation 8 a-e.

The catalytic rate constants were presented in the order 4.36 × 102 > 1.94 × 102 > 1.83 × 102 > 5.99 × 101 > 1.87 × 101 for MnTAPP@GQDs, MnTAPP--GQDs, MnTAPP, GQDs and TAPP respectively (Table 1). MnTAPP@GQDs showed the highest catalytic rate constant followed by MnTAPP--GQDs. Although MnTAPP--GQDs showed better charge transfer in term of peak separation
Δ
Ep and Rct, and surface coverage, MnTAPP@GQDs gave a higher rate constant for hydrazine oxidation. It is important to note that electrocatalytic activity does not only depend on the charge transfer, the interaction of the analyte with the electrode modifiers also influences electrocatalytic activity. The central metal where hydrazine links, could be more exposed in MnTAPP@GQDs than in MnTAPP—GQDs. The catalytic rate constants of MnTAPP--GQDs and MnTAPP@GQDs were higher than that of MnTAPP because of the effects of GQDs. The central metal introduction is also reported to improve the catalytic rate [40]. The higher catalytic rate constant for MnTAPP@GQDs compared to MnTAPP--GQDs, could be due to the covalent linkage of the amino group of the porphyrin and the carboxylic group of the quantum dots. Such covalent bonding improves the sharing of the π electron system of MnTAPP and GQDs. TAPP performed the worst.

The limit of detection (LoD) for the detection of hydrazine using the modified electrodes (insert in Fig. S6a) was calculated by using Eqn.9.
(9)
LoD =
3 σ
slope
where
σ
represents the standard deviation.

The LoD was found to be 0.0023 mM < 0.0042 mM < 0.011 mM < 0.017 mM < 0.018 mM for MnTAPP@GQDs, MnTAPP--GQDs, MnTAPP, TAPP, and GQDs as shown in Table 1. The MnTAPP@GQDs showed thea lowest LoD. The second lowest LoD was obtained from MnTAPP--GQDs which could be caused by the
π
-
π
interaction between GQDs and the porphyrin. MnTAPP also showed better LoD compared to TAPP and this could be due to the effect of manganese as a central metal. The detection limits obtained in this study are lower than the values previously reported in some cases [10,44,45].

Ammonia (0.1 mM) and aniline (0.1 mM) were explored as potential interfering analytes in the presence of hydrazine (0.025 mM) in the potential rage 0–1 V using square wave voltammogram (Fig. S7). These compounds have a potential to interfere with hydrazine [46]. Interestingly there was no response found in ammonia, however aniline showed a response at 0.55 V. In the presence of ammonia, there was a 8.42% increase in the current for hydrazine oxidation and a small shift towards the positive potentials. A decrease of 13.54% was observed for hydrazine peak in the presence of aniline and a very small shift towards the positive potentials. However, a decrease of 42.61% was observed in the presence of all analytes and a small shift towards the positive potentials. Moreover, in the presence of aniline and in the mixture of all analytes, the sensor was able to show two separate responses for hydrazine and aniline at different potentials. These results showed us that although the current is significantly affected by the presence of the interfering analytes used here, the sensor is still selective and specific.

The introduction of Mn in the center of tetra-4-aminophenyl porphyrin resulted to an improved electrochemical behavior towards the detection of hydrazine. Attachment of the MnTAPP to the GQDs improved the detection limit of hydrazine and also improved the catalytic activity. MnTAPP showed the lowest hydrazine peak potential of 0.65 V. MnTAPP@GQDs also showed better catalytic rate constant of 4.36 × 102 Ms−1 and lowest LoD of 0.0023 mM followed by MnTAPP--GQDs. Furthermore, the sensor showed good selectivity in the presence of interfering analytes. All probes showed good stability.

Mbulelo Jokazi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lekhetho S. Mpeta: Methodology, Data curation. Tebello Nyokong: Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Institute for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2021.115748.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
